rūḥ.key_passage: The origin of this dispute is to be found in the dispute between the nafs and the ruh, for these are two companions who reside in every human at all times; until either the nafs dies or the ruh dies, they will remain companions and no human will be free of their contesting with each other.
rūḥ.key_ar: وأصل هذا الخلاف من الخلاف الذي بین النفس والروح فهما قرینان في كل إنسان دائما
rūḥ.key_point: The rūḥ and nafs are permanent companions in every human, in continuous contest. For the rūḥ to be alive and the nafs dead — that is real life.
rūḥ.source: Muḥammad al-Mishri, Qanābil Yadawiyya fī al-Dhabb ʿan Ṭarīq al-Ṣūfiyya, trans. Saʿeed Simone Nardella & Adnan Wood-Smith (n.p., 2018)

qalb.key_passage: The nafs and the ruh only come into agreement in the heart of the perfect wali, to whom they become submissive; the Iman of the nafs and the ruh will never be complete until the two of them have the wali decide between them whenever they dispute.
qalb.key_ar: النفس والروح لا تتفقان إلا في قلب الولي الكامل
qalb.key_point: The qalb is the locus where rūḥ and nafs are reconciled — exclusively in the heart of the complete wali.
qalb.source: Muḥammad al-Mishri, Qanābil Yadawiyya fī al-Dhabb ʿan Ṭarīq al-Ṣūfiyya, trans. Saʿeed Simone Nardella & Adnan Wood-Smith (n.p., 2018)

sulūk.key_passage: When jadhb is complete and they travel the path of suluk, they see the ayah in front of them indicating suluk, the multiplicity of haqa'iq, the distinction of nisbahs, and dispersion. For each new maqam the saliks reach in any hadrah, they find the ayah ahead of them translating for them what they are currently experiencing.
sulūk.key_ar: كلما ازداد فهم اﷲ ازداد فهم الآیة ولا نهایة لفهم الاثنین
sulūk.key_point: Sulūk is the path of traversal through the maqāmāt within the ḥaḍarāt — each station accompanied by a corresponding understanding of the Qurʾān.
sulūk.source: Muḥammad al-Mishri, Qanābil Yadawiyya fī al-Dhabb ʿan Ṭarīq al-Ṣūfiyya, trans. Saʿeed Simone Nardella & Adnan Wood-Smith (n.p., 2018)

maqām.key_passage: If the wali speaks from the maqam of selfless slavehood and uses attributes of deficiency to describe him- or herself, the denier has no choice but to say, 'He just said what he did to show everyone how pious he is.'
maqām.key_ar: إذا كان الولي في مقام العبودة وترجم عن نفسه بصفات النقص لا بد أن یقول المنكر هذا قاله لیظهر تواضعه
maqām.key_point: The maqām determines the mode of speech. Speaking from the maqām of slavehood (ʿubūda) means using attributes of deficiency — which the uninitiated misread as false humility.
maqām.source: Muḥammad al-Mishri, Qanābil Yadawiyya fī al-Dhabb ʿan Ṭarīq al-Ṣūfiyya, trans. Saʿeed Simone Nardella & Adnan Wood-Smith (n.p., 2018)

tarbiya.key_passage: I woke up and went to Shaykh Ibrahim, who renewed the wird for me, and I received tarbiyah at his hands.
tarbiya.key_ar: فذهبت إلى الشیخ إبراهیم فجدد لي الورد وتربیت على یدیه
tarbiya.key_point: Mishri's personal testimony: tarbiya is received directly at the hands of the Shaykh — it is the initiatic transmission of the spiritual method.
tarbiya.source: Muḥammad al-Mishri, Qanābil Yadawiyya fī al-Dhabb ʿan Ṭarīq al-Ṣūfiyya, trans. Saʿeed Simone Nardella & Adnan Wood-Smith (n.p., 2018)

fayḍa.key_passage: ...the owner of the Faydah that Shaykh Tijani had mentioned, in addition to other matters that neither pen nor paper could do justice.
fayḍa.key_ar: صاحب الفیضة التي ذكر الشیخ التجاني
fayḍa.key_point: Mishri witnesses Niasse as the ṣāḥib al-fayḍa — the one the Tijānī founder predicted. This is Mishri's founding act of recognition.
fayḍa.source: Muḥammad al-Mishri, Qanābil Yadawiyya fī al-Dhabb ʿan Ṭarīq al-Ṣūfiyya, trans. Saʿeed Simone Nardella & Adnan Wood-Smith (n.p., 2018)

wilāya.key_passage: The first step a wali takes in walaya is the killing of the nafs. Then he struggles against other nufus... The people of nufus are regimented soldiers who know their enemies and are constantly striving against anyone [who threatens their dominance].
wilāya.key_ar: أول قدم عنده في الولایة هو قتل النفس ثم یجاهد الأنفس
wilāya.key_point: Wilāya begins with self-mortification (killing the nafs) then extends to spiritual combat on behalf of others.
wilāya.source: Muḥammad al-Mishri, Qanābil Yadawiyya fī al-Dhabb ʿan Ṭarīq al-Ṣūfiyya, trans. Saʿeed Simone Nardella & Adnan Wood-Smith (n.p., 2018)
